sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Matthew, presented with right knee pain that started after playing basketball when he landed awkwardly and felt his knee pop, leading to swelling and difficulty walking.
He reports the pain is achy and has been present since the injury, with some improvement overnight.
He has taken Tylenol with some relief.
He rates his pain level at 4/10.
His past medical history includes hypertension and diabetes, with an A1c of 7.2.
He is currently taking 5mg of Norvasc daily for hypertension and 500mg of metformin daily for diabetes, but has been inconsistent with taking his medications.
He has a history of a grade 2 systolic ejection murmur, which he has had for a couple of years.
Review of systems: The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes, and his blood pressure is currently elevated at 140/80.
Current medications: Norvasc 5mg daily for hypertension; Metformin 500mg daily for diabetes; Tylenol (dose not specified) for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 140/80.
Physical exam: The patient has pain to palpation of the medial aspect of the right knee, with some edema, swelling, and ecchymosis.
The varus and valgus tests were negative.
The knee exam also showed some swelling and redness.
Test Results: X-ray of the right knee was normal, showing no fractures or bony abnormalities.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an MCL strain or possible tear, which will require an MRI to confirm.
Problem list: Hypertension; Diabetes; MCL strain/suspected tear; Grade 2 systolic ejection murmur (past).
Plan:
I prescribed Meloxicam 15mg daily for pain and swelling, and a knee immobilizer to be worn for the next couple of weeks.
The patient will continue to take Tylenol as needed.
I will also prescribe a log book for the patient to track their daily blood sugars, and will get a consult with nutrition to improve their diet.
I will up the patient's Norvasc medication to 10mg daily to help lower their blood pressure.
I will see the patient back in two weeks for follow-up.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in two weeks for a follow-up appointment.
